title: The Two Pots
lines: Two Pots lay together in a corner of a kitchen.
One was made of brass and shone brightly.
The other was made of earthenware and looked very ordinary.
"You are so beautiful," said the earthen pot to the brass pot.
"I wish I were as bright and shining as you."
"I can assure you," replied the brass pot, "that I was once as plain and dull as you are now."
"But I have been rubbed and polished so much that I have become bright and beautiful."
"What does it matter how you look?" asked the earthen pot.
"If you are put on the fire, you will be burned."
"But I, though I may crack or break, will be of use as long as I last."
Even the plainest things may have their value.
moral: True worth lies in usefulness, not in appearance.
